slides.title: Unmasking AI Bias
slides.subtitle: Understanding, Detecting, and Mitigating Bias in Artificial Intelligence
slides.content: A comprehensive guide to identifying and addressing bias in AI systems through visual learning and interactive exploration. This module covers the fundamentals of AI bias, its sources, real-world impacts, and practical mitigation strategies.

slides.title: What is AI Bias?
slides.content: AI bias refers to systematic and repeatable errors in a computer system that create unfair outcomes, privileging one arbitrary group of users over others. Unlike random errors, bias creates consistent patterns that disadvantage certain populations.
bulletPoints: Systematic errors that disadvantage certain groups
Can be intentional or unintentional
Present in data, algorithms, and human oversight
Affects decision-making across all domains
Often reflects and amplifies existing societal inequalities

quote.text: Algorithms are opinions embedded in code.
quote.author: Cathy O'Neil, Author of Weapons of Math Destruction

slides.title: Types of AI Bias
slides.content: AI bias manifests in multiple forms throughout the development and deployment lifecycle. Understanding these types helps identify where interventions are needed.

slides.title: Sources of Bias
slides.content: Understanding where bias originates is crucial for effective mitigation strategies. Each source requires different approaches to address.

sections.title: Historical Data
sections.content: Training data reflects past societal biases and inequalities, perpetuating historical discrimination into AI systems

sections.title: Sampling Bias
sections.content: Underrepresentation of certain groups in training datasets leads to poor performance for those populations

sections.title: Feature Selection
sections.content: Choosing features that correlate with protected attributes can introduce proxy discrimination

sections.title: Label Bias
sections.content: Human annotators introduce subjective judgments and stereotypes during the labeling process

sections.title: Confirmation Bias
sections.content: Developers may unconsciously select data and metrics that confirm their existing beliefs

slides.title: Real-World Impact of AI Bias
slides.content: Documented cases of AI bias affecting millions of people across different sectors. These numbers represent reported incidents and likely underrepresent the true scale.

slides.title: Famous Bias Cases
slides.content: Several high-profile cases have exposed the real-world consequences of AI bias, leading to regulatory scrutiny and calls for accountability.

sections.title: COMPAS Recidivism Algorithm
sections.content: Used by US courts, it incorrectly labeled Black defendants as 'high risk' at nearly twice the rate of white defendants

sections.title: Amazon Hiring Algorithm
sections.content: Trained on historical data, it systematically downgraded resumes from women for technical positions

sections.title: Google Photos
sections.content: Image classification algorithm labeled Black people as gorillas, highlighting flaws in training data

sections.title: Apple Card
sections.content: Credit limit algorithm exhibited gender bias, giving significantly lower limits to women despite similar creditworthiness

slides.title: The BBQ Benchmark
slides.content: The Bias Benchmark for Question Answering (BBQ) is a comprehensive dataset designed to evaluate social biases in question answering models across multiple demographic categories.
bulletPoints: 11 bias categories including age, gender, race, religion, and more
58,492 examples with ambiguous and disambiguated contexts
Tests for stereotyping and bias alignment in model responses
Measures accuracy and bias scores separately for comprehensive evaluation
Provides standardized metrics for comparing model fairness

slides.title: BBQ Bias Categories Distribution
slides.content: Distribution of examples across different bias categories in the BBQ dataset. The largest categories focus on intersectional biases (race × gender, race × SES).

slides.title: Bias in NLP Models
slides.content: Large Language Models (LLMs) can exhibit various forms of bias in their text generation, reflecting patterns learned from training data.
bulletPoints: Stereotypical associations between demographic groups and professions
Gender bias in pronoun resolution and job-related contexts
Racial and ethnic bias in sentiment analysis
Religious bias in character associations
Age-related bias in workplace scenarios

sections.title: Representation Bias
sections.content: Underrepresented groups in training data lead to poor model performance for those populations

sections.title: Performance Bias
sections.content: Models perform differently across demographic groups, even when accuracy is similar overall

sections.title: Aggregation Bias
sections.content: One-size-fits-all models fail to capture group-specific patterns

slides.title: Mitigation Strategies
slides.content: Effective approaches to reduce bias in AI systems throughout the development lifecycle. A multi-layered approach is most effective.

sections.title: Data Preprocessing
sections.content: Balance datasets, remove sensitive attributes, augment underrepresented groups, and apply fairness constraints

sections.title: Algorithmic Fairness
sections.content: Implement fairness constraints, adversarial debiasing, and regularization techniques during training

sections.title: Post-Processing
sections.content: Adjust model outputs to ensure equalized odds and demographic parity across groups

sections.title: Continuous Monitoring
sections.content: Track model performance across demographic groups in production with automated alerts

slides.title: Fairness Metrics Comparison
slides.content: Different fairness metrics measure various aspects of algorithmic fairness. This radar chart shows improvement after applying mitigation strategies.

slides.title: Fairness Metrics Explained
slides.content: Understanding different fairness metrics helps choose the right approach for your specific use case. Each metric has trade-offs.

sections.title: Demographic Parity
sections.content: Ensure positive outcomes are equally likely across demographic groups, regardless of qualifications

sections.title: Equal Opportunity
sections.content: Ensure equally qualified individuals have equal chances of positive outcomes

sections.title: Equalized Odds
sections.content: True positive and false positive rates should be equal across groups for qualified individuals

sections.title: Predictive Parity
sections.content: Predictions should have similar accuracy across demographic groups

slides.title: Best Practices & Recommendations
slides.content: Key takeaways for building fair and unbiased AI systems. Implementing these practices helps create more equitable technology.
bulletPoints: Conduct regular bias audits using standardized benchmarks like BBQ
Diversify development teams to bring multiple perspectives to design
Document data sources and potential biases transparently for stakeholders
Establish clear fairness metrics relevant to your specific use case
Implement human-in-the-loop oversight for high-stakes decisions
Create feedback mechanisms for affected communities to report issues
Use explainable AI techniques to understand model decisions

slides.title: Your Role in AI Fairness
slides.content: Everyone has a role to play in creating fair AI systems. From developers to policymakers to end users, collective action drives change.

sections.title: For Developers
sections.content: Prioritize fairness from the start, not as an afterthought. Use bias detection tools and follow ethical guidelines.

sections.title: For Organizations
sections.content: Establish AI ethics committees, conduct regular audits, and invest in diverse teams.

sections.title: For Policymakers
sections.content: Create regulations that require transparency and accountability in AI systems.

sections.title: For Everyone
sections.content: Stay informed, ask questions, and advocate for responsible AI development.
